summary: English Romantic poet and Poet Laureate, co-author of Lyrical Ballads with Coleridge. Known for The Prelude, 'Tintern Abbey,' 'I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud,' and his nature poetry rooted in the Lake District.

works.name: The Prelude
works.description: An autobiographical poem in blank verse, subtitled 'Growth of a Poet's Mind,' published posthumously. It recounts Wordsworth's childhood experiences including skating, boat-stealing, and the 'spots of time.' The 1805 version differs from the 1850 version. It includes the ascent of Mount Snowdon and reflections on the French Revolution. The famous opening describes 'a correspondent breeze.'

clues.clue: Subtitled 'Growth of a Poet's Mind'; autobiographical poem in blank verse
examples: this autobiographical poem subtitled 'Growth of a Poet's Mind'

clues.clue: Childhood episodes: skating, boat-stealing, 'spots of time'
Ascent of Mount Snowdon; reflections on French Revolution

works.name: Tintern Abbey
works.description: Full title 'Lines Composed a Few Miles above Tintern Abbey, on Revisiting the Banks of the Wye During a Tour, July 13, 1798.' The speaker returns after five years and reflects on nature's power to restore the spirit. Contains the phrase 'sense sublime / Of something far more deeply interfused.' Addressed to the speaker's sister Dorothy. Part of Lyrical Ballads.

clues.clue: Speaker returns after five years to the banks of the Wye
examples: revisiting the banks of the Wye

clues.clue: 'A sense sublime / Of something far more deeply interfused'
examples: sense sublime / Of something far more deeply interfused

clues.clue: Addressed to the speaker's sister Dorothy

works.name: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud (Daffodils)
works.description: One of Wordsworth's most famous poems, about encountering a 'host, of golden daffodils' beside a lake. The speaker recalls them later: 'they flash upon that inward eye / Which is the bliss of solitude.' His sister Dorothy's journal entry about the same walk reportedly influenced the poem.

clues.clue: A 'host of golden daffodils' beside a lake
'They flash upon that inward eye / Which is the bliss of solitude'
examples: they flash upon that inward eye

works.name: London, 1802
works.description: A sonnet addressing John Milton: 'Milton! thou shouldst be living at this hour' because England 'is a fen / Of stagnant waters.' Wordsworth laments England's moral decline.

clues.clue: 'Milton! thou shouldst be living at this hour'; England 'is a fen / Of stagnant waters'
examples: this person is told, 'Thou shouldst be living at this hour' in William Wordsworth's 'London, 1802'

works.name: Other Works and General
works.description: Wordsworth co-authored Lyrical Ballads (1798) with Coleridge, which included a famous Preface defining poetry as 'the spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings.' He was Poet Laureate (1843-1850). He wrote 'Ode: Intimations of Immortality' ('The Child is father of the Man'; 'trailing clouds of glory'). Jonathan Bate's Romantic Ecology is a foundational ecocriticism work about Wordsworth. He wrote 'The Solitary Reaper,' 'Michael,' 'Resolution and Independence' (the Leech-Gatherer), and 'We Are Seven.' He lived in the Lake District and was one of the 'Lake Poets.' His sister Dorothy was an important companion and diarist. He wrote 'The World Is Too Much with Us' ('Getting and spending, we lay waste our powers').

clues.clue: Co-authored Lyrical Ballads with Coleridge; Preface defines poetry as 'spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings'
'Ode: Intimations of Immortality' — 'The Child is father of the Man'; 'trailing clouds of glory'
Jonathan Bate's Romantic Ecology (1991) — foundational ecocriticism about Wordsworth

comprehensive_summary:
William Wordsworth was an English Romantic poet who served as Poet Laureate from 1843 to 1850. With Samuel Taylor Coleridge, he co-authored Lyrical Ballads (1798), whose famous Preface defined poetry as 'the spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings.' He is one of the most frequently clued Romantic poets.

The Prelude, subtitled 'Growth of a Poet's Mind,' is Wordsworth's major autobiographical poem in blank verse, published posthumously. It recounts childhood experiences including skating, boat-stealing, and 'spots of time,' and includes the ascent of Mount Snowdon and reflections on the French Revolution.

'Lines Composed a Few Miles above Tintern Abbey' describes the speaker returning after five years to the banks of the Wye, finding 'a sense sublime / Of something far more deeply interfused.' It is addressed to his sister Dorothy.

'I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud' describes encountering 'a host of golden daffodils' beside a lake; later 'they flash upon that inward eye / Which is the bliss of solitude.' 'London, 1802' addresses Milton: 'Milton! thou shouldst be living at this hour' because England 'is a fen / Of stagnant waters.'

'Ode: Intimations of Immortality' contains 'The Child is father of the Man' and 'trailing clouds of glory.' 'The World Is Too Much with Us' laments 'Getting and spending, we lay waste our powers.' Other poems include 'The Solitary Reaper,' 'Michael,' and 'Resolution and Independence' (the Leech-Gatherer). Jonathan Bate's Romantic Ecology is a foundational ecocriticism work about Wordsworth. He lived in the Lake District and was one of the 'Lake Poets' with Coleridge and Southey. His sister Dorothy was an important companion and diarist.
